title-group.article-title: Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies for people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease

sec.title: Rationale
sec.p: Alzheimer’s disease is a neurodegenerative disorder and the most common cause of dementia. Aggregated amyloid‐beta protein deposits are implicated in its pathogenesis. Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies (sometimes represented as Aβ‐mAbs) are potentially disease‐modifying for Alzheimer’s disease: through the clearance of amyloid in the brain, they may slow cognitive and functional decline.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To assess the clinical benefits and harms of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies aducanumab, bapineuzumab, crenezumab, donanemab, gantenerumab, lecanemab, ponezumab, remternetug, and solanezumab in people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease.

sec.title: Search methods
sec.p: We searched CENTRAL, MEDLINE (PubMed), Embase, and two clinical trials registries (Clinicaltrials.gov and WHO International Clinical Trials Registry Platform), and we undertook reference checking and citation research. The most recent search date was 7 August 2025.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: We included randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that lasted at least 12 months and compared amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies with placebo or no treatment in people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease. We included both parallel‐group and cluster designs.

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: Our outcomes of critical importance were: cognitive function; dementia severity; functional ability; any amyloid‐related imaging abnormality (ARIA), which includes oedema (E) and haemorrhage (H); any symptomatic ARIA E and H; symptomatic brain haemorrhage; serious adverse events; and any‐cause mortality. We analysed data at 12, 18, 24, and over 24 months of treatment.

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: We used the Cochrane risk of bias tool RoB 2 to assess the risk of bias in outcomes of critical importance.

sec.title: Synthesis methods
sec.p: We meta‐analysed results for each outcome within each comparison using the inverse variance method and the random‐effects model. We used GRADE to assess the certainty of evidence for each outcome as very low, low, moderate, or high.

sec.title: Included studies
sec.p: Overall, we included 17 studies with 20,342 participants. The mean age of participants in the studies ranged from 70 to 74 years. Seven studies enroled only participants with mild dementia, and one study enroled only participants with mild cognitive impairment. The remaining studies included a mixed population. The mean duration of participants' cognitive impairment ranged from 17 to 52 months.

The 17 studies assessed seven different amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies: aducanumab (n = 3), bapineuzumab (n = 4), crenezumab (n = 2), donanemab (n = 1), gantenerumab (n = 4), lecanemab (n = 1), and solanezumab (n = 2). All used placebo as a comparison. Eleven studies lasted 18 months, four lasted 24 months, and two lasted more than 24 months.
All studies were funded by the pharmaceutical industry.

sec.title: Synthesis of results
sec.p: Below, we report the results of the studies at 18 months.

p.bold: Cognitive function

sec.p: Compared to placebo, amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies probably result in little to no difference in cognitive function as measured by the ADAS‐Cog (Alzheimer's Disease Assessment Scale‐Cognitive) scale (standardised mean difference (SMD) −0.11, 95% confidence interval (CI) −0.16 to −0.06; 13 studies, 9895 participants; moderate certainty).

p.bold: Dementia severity

sec.p: Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies may result in little to no difference in dementia severity as measured by the CDR‐SB (Clincal Dementia Rating Sum of Boxes) scale (SMD −0.12, 95% CI −0.24 to −0.00; 9 studies, 8053 participants; low certainty).

p.bold: Functional ability

sec.p: Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies probably result in little to no difference in functional ability as measured on the ADCS‐ADL (Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study ‐ Activities of Daily Living) scale (SMD 0.09, 95% CI 0.03 to 0.16; 3 studies, 3478 participants; moderate certainty) and may result in a small increase in functional ability if measured with the ADCS‐iADL (Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study‐Instrumental Activities of Daily Living) scale (SMD 0.21, 95% CI 0.10 to 0.32; 1 study, 1252 participants; low certainty) or ADCS‐ADL‐MCI (Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study ‐ Activities of Daily Living for Mild Cognitive Impairment) scale (SMD 0.23, 95% CI 0.12 to 0.33; 4 studies, 2802 participants; low certainty).

p.bold: Adverse events

sec.p: Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies probably result in a small increase in the occurrence of any ARIA E (ARD (absolute risk difference) 107 more per 1000, 95% CI 77 more to 148 more; 11 studies, 13,595 participants; moderate certainty) and probably little to no difference in symptomatic ARIA E (ARD 29 more per 1000, 95% CI 22 more to 38 more; 2 studies, 3522 participants; moderate certainty) or symptomatic ARIA H (ARD 4 more per 1000, 95% CI 1 fewer to 31 more; 1 study, 1795 participants; moderate certainty).
Three studies assessing any ARIA H showed heterogeneous results (I2 = 81%), which prevented pooled analysis.

At 18 months, amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies do not increase serious adverse events (ARD 6 more events per 1000, 95% CI 10 fewer to 26 more; 9 studies, 11,904 participants; high certainty) or overall mortality (ARD 2 more events per 1000, 95% CI 3 fewer to 11 more; 7 studies, 9733 participants; high certainty).

We judged the overall risk of bias as low for the outcomes of serious adverse events and mortality. We had some concerns about the overall risk of bias for efficacy outcomes, mainly due to the risk of functional unblinding (i.e. participants and investigators correctly guessing whether a participant is receiving the active drug or placebo because of noticeable side effects).

sec.title: Authors' conclusions
sec.p: The effect of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies on cognitive function and dementia severity at 18 months in people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease is trivial, while on functional ability, it is small at best. Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies increase the risk of amyloid‐related imaging abnormalities. Both desirable outcomes and adverse events were inconsistently reported in the studies included in the review.

Successful removal of amyloid from the brain does not seem to be associated with clinically meaningful effects in people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease. Future research on disease‐modifying treatments for Alzheimer’s disease should focus on other mechanisms of action.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This Cochrane review was funded in part by the Drug and Medical Devices Governance Area, Regione Emilia‐Romagna, Bologna, Italy.
The publication of this article was supported by "Ricerca Corrente" funding from the Italian Ministry of Health.

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: Protocol (2025): PROSPERO registration number CRD420251114325

abstract.title: Plain language summary

p.bold: Are medicines (anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies) that reduce the build‐up of abnormal proteins in the brain an effective treatment for people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease, and do they cause unwanted effects?
Key messages

list-item.p: In people with mild memory and thinking problems (mild cognitive impairment (MCI)) or mild dementia due to Alzheimer's disease, laboratory‐produced medicines (anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies) that target and remove potentially damaging build‐ups of amyloid proteins in the brain, probably result in little to no difference in the decline in memory functioning and thinking ability, or in how severe dementia symptoms are, compared with placebo (sham treatment) 18 months after the start of treatment.
Anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies probably cause more brain swelling and tiny (micro) bleeds than placebo. They do not increase other serious unwanted effects or deaths compared with placebo.

Successful removal of amyloid proteins from the brain does not seem to be associated with clinically meaningful improvements in people with MCI or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease. Future research on disease‐modifying treatments for Alzheimer’s disease should focus on other treatments.

p.bold: What is Alzheimer’s disease?

abstract.p: In Alzheimer’s disease, brain cells die following the build‐up of proteins (called amyloid plaques). Alzheimer’s disease affects people’s memory and thinking abilities. Symptoms are usually mild to begin with and do not interfere with everyday life. This is called ‘mild cognitive impairment’ (MCI). Over time, it can progress to mild dementia, where memory and thinking difficulties are serious enough to interfere with everyday activities. About 15% of people with MCI will develop dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease within two years. It is the most common form of dementia among older people.

p.bold: What are anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies?

abstract.p: Antibodies are made by the body as a defence against disease. They can also be produced in a laboratory for use as a medical treatment. Anti‐amyloid antibodies are designed to target the amyloid proteins that cause plaques due to Alzheimer’s disease, and remove them from the brain. They are ‘monoclonal’ because they only target amyloid proteins. Removing amyloid proteins from the brain may slow the progression of Alzheimer’s disease.

p.bold: What did we want to find out?

abstract.p: We wanted to know if anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies are an effective medicine for people with MCI or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease. We evaluated whether they slowed down:

list-item.p: the decline in memory and thinking;
the decline in ability to manage everyday activities; and
the worsening of dementia symptoms.

abstract.p: We also wanted to know if they caused any unwanted effects.

p.bold: What did we do?

abstract.p: We searched for studies that investigated one or more anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies to treat people with MCI or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease, compared with placebo (sham treatment that does not contain any medicine but looks identical to the medicine being tested and is delivered in the same way).
We summarised the results of the studies, and rated our confidence in the evidence, considering aspects such as study sizes and methods.

p.bold: What did we find?

abstract.p: We found 17 studies that were carried out in different countries and involved 20,342 people. The average age across studies was from 70 to 74 years. All studies were funded by companies that produced the anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies.

p.bold: Main results

abstract.p: After 18 months of treatment, anti‐amyloid monoclonal antibodies:

list-item.p: may make little to no difference to how bad people’s dementia symptoms are (9 studies, 8053 people);
probably make little to no difference in the decline in memory and thinking ability (13 studies, 9895 people) or the ability to manage everyday activities (3 studies, 3478 people);
may result in a small improvement in more complex everyday tasks, such as shopping, managing finances, taking medication, and using transportation (1 study, 1252 people);

probably result in a small increase in the occurrence of brain swelling. For every 1000 people using monoclonal antibodies, 119 developed brain swelling compared with only 12 of 1000 people using placebo (11 studies, 13,595 people);
may result in a small increase in microbleeds in the brain (3 studies, 4308 people);
do not increase other serious unwanted effects as defined by the study authors (9 studies, 11,904 people); and
do not increase deaths from any cause (7 studies, 9733 people).

p.bold: What are the limitations of the evidence?

abstract.p: Our confidence in the evidence is limited for two reasons. Firstly, people who received monoclonal antibodies had more brain swelling and microbleeds than people receiving placebo. However, most studies did not separate people with symptoms of brain swelling and microbleeds from those in whom these effects were only visible with a scan. This reporting gap leaves patients without the information they need to understand the seriousness of potential unwanted effects. Secondly, the results came from studies that did not last very long. These are important limitations in the evidence for people with Alzheimer’s disease, who need to know the longer‐term benefits and unwanted effects of medicines.
We found six ongoing studies. The review's conclusions may change as new results become available.

p.bold: How up to date is this evidence?

abstract.p: The evidence is current to 7 August 2025.

GRADE Working Group grades of evidence High certainty: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect. Moderate certainty: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different. Low certainty: our confidence in the effect estimate is limited: the true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect. Very low certainty: we have very little confidence in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect.

sec.title: Description of the condition
sec.p: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the leading cause of dementia, affecting 57.4 million people worldwide [1]. Epidemiological data in the last decade highlighted a sharp increase in AD incidence, which has increased from 26.6 million people worldwide in 2006, and is predicted to increase to about 106 million by 2050 [2]. Notably, 68% of the increase is expected to be localised in low‐ and middle‐income countries [3]. The disease is clinically characterised by progressive cognitive and functional decline, leading to progressive loss of independence and increasing care needs. The typical age of onset is over 65 years, and prevalence rates increase with increasing age [4].

Clinically, AD evolves and is on a continuum, starting with a subjective report of cognitive decline followed by mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and progressing to mild, moderate, and severe dementia [5]. MCI due to AD is defined as objective cognitive decline (usually memory difficulties predominantly), while retaining independence in functional abilities [6]. MCI is often seen as a transitional stage between normal cognition and dementia. Among those with MCI, about 15% develop dementia after two years and about one third develop dementia due to AD within five years [7]. However, many individuals with MCI do not have additional cognitive decline or revert to normal cognition. A systematic review and meta‐analysis reported a reversion rate of 26% in population‐based studies [8]. In other words, MCI does not always lead inevitably to dementia, and recovery is possible in some cases [9].
If dementia does develop, the next stage is mild dementia, which is characterised by cognitive decline that interferes with activities of daily living [10].

While the diagnosis of “definite AD” can be performed only post‐mortem on neuropathological grounds, “possible” or “probable” AD and its severity staging can be diagnosed in living people, primarily by means of clinical evaluation and supported by neuroimaging techniques (magnetic resonance imaging, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) investigation, and amyloid positron emission tomography (PET)).

Two conceptual models—one linear and one nonlinear—have been proposed to explain the transition to clinical AD, but they remain theoretical and confined to research settings, and are not yet applied in routine clinical practice. Further exploration to understand their relevance to disease progression and possible reversibility is warranted [11].

Biomarkers reflecting AD pathology, such as tau accumulation, measurable neurodegeneration, dynamic neurochemical alterations, can be detected in vivo in many healthy older persons without symptoms. These people will not all develop dementia. Therefore, the relevance of such biomarkers of disease without clinical symptoms is uncertain.

Both modifiable and non‐modifiable risk factors contribute to the development of AD. Non‐modifiable factors include age, genetic predisposition (particularly the presence of the APOE ε4 allele), and family history. Modifiable risk factors, representing potential targets for prevention strategies, include vascular risk factors (such as hypertension, diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia), low educational attainment, physical inactivity, smoking, depression, social isolation, and hearing loss [12].

The pathological hallmarks of AD include extracellular deposition of aggregated amyloid‐beta protein (Aβ) and intracellular tangles of hyperphosphorylated tau protein [13], along with synaptic and neuronal loss [14]. These changes lead to network dysfunction and progressive cognitive impairment. The first lesions are believed to start accumulating 10 to 15 years before the onset of cognitive symptoms.
The amyloid cascade hypothesis posits that amyloid‐beta accumulation is an early and pivotal event in AD pathogenesis, triggering downstream tauopathy, neurodegeneration, and clinical symptoms [15].
Currently, no treatment is available to stop the natural course of AD towards progressive disability.

Current treatments for AD include drug therapies, such as cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine, and cognition‐oriented treatments. Cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine exert a symptomatic action in people with AD by increasing the bioavailability of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine within cholinergic pathways disrupted by the disease and by slowing neurotoxicity processes, respectively. Both have a minimal to moderate effect [16, 17, 18, 19]. Cognition‐oriented treatments, including cognitive stimulation, cognitive training, and cognitive rehabilitation, are non‐pharmacological interventions aimed at slowing cognitive decline by engaging people with mild dementia in activities stimulating thinking, concentration, and memory [20].

AD represents a major global health challenge, with a significant social and economic impact on health systems. Progressive loss of functional independence by people with AD implies an increasing need for support by caregivers, with a significant health and psychosocial impact on families and society. This burden is expected to increase with the ageing population and the rising prevalence of AD, placing a strain on healthcare systems.

sec.title: Description of the intervention and how it might work
sec.p: Based on the amyloid cascade hypothesis, amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies (Aβ‐mAbs) have been developed as potential disease‐modifying therapies for AD [21], that is, they can potentially slow the disease, although they cannot cure it. The mechanism of action relates to binding to various forms of amyloid‐beta—monomeric, oligomeric, or fibrillar—and promoting clearance via microglial phagocytosis or peripheral mechanisms [22, 23]. The rationale behind the clinical use of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies is that reducing amyloid‐beta plaque burden and mitigating downstream neurodegenerative processes may slow cognitive and functional decline [24].

Clinical trials have evaluated the effects of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies in people with MCI or mild dementia due to AD, that is, in the early symptomatic stages of AD when, it is hypothesised, amyloid‐beta‐targeting interventions might be most effective in slowing disease progression [25].

Several clinical studies using repeated amyloid PET scanning have shown that the use of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies can lead to a reduction of aggregated amyloid‐beta in the brain, although the clinical impact of such findings is uncertain, with mixed results from RCTs, and poor correlation between amyloid removal and changes in cognitive function [26, 27].

First‐generation amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies targeting the monomeric amyloid‐beta, such as solanezumab, bapineuzumab, and crenezumab, failed to demonstrate clinical benefit for AD in clinical trials. In contrast, second‐generation amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies, including aducanumab, lecanemab, donanemab, and gantenerumab directed against pathogenic amyloid‐beta species and aggregates have shown mixed results, with some RCTs showing small differences in favour of the intervention arm on cognitive parameters after 18 months of treatment.

Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies include aducanumab, bapinezumab, crenezumab, donanemab, gantenerumab, lecanemab, ponezumab, and solanezumab. They are administered intravenously, typically every two to four weeks, with dosage and administration protocols varying across agents. The approval by regulatory agencies of aducanumab [28], donanemab [29], and lecanemab [30, 31], represents a turning point in AD treatment. However, their net clinical benefit for people with AD is debated, with small effect sizes on cognition and function in pivotal trials paired with frequent occurrence of potentially serious adverse effects such as amyloid‐related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), including ARIA E, which refers to vasogenic oedema or effusions, and ARIA H, which indicates micro‐haemorrhagic events, as well as infusion reactions and other adverse events [32]. Moreover, some argue that the reported desirable effects may be the result of bias due to the functional unblinding that occurs when participants and investigators correctly guess whether a participant is receiving the active drug or placebo because of apparent adverse effects [33, 34, 35].

Patients at higher risk of developing ARIA include those carrying the APOE ε4 allele —especially homozygotes—as well as individuals with a high load of cerebral microbleeds at baseline, presumably reflecting amyloid angiopathy. Risk also appears to increase with higher antibody doses and more rapid titration schedules. These side effects can limit dosage and treatment duration [32].

Furthermore, as mentioned, the drugs require repeated intravenous administration and close monitoring through neuroimaging assessments, with consequent organisational and economic implications adding to the cost of the medication.

sec.title: Why it is important to do this review
sec.p: The net benefit in clinical practice of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies is debated in the scientific community due to uncertainty after weighing the limited clinical relevance of efficacy outcomes against potentially severe adverse events, which are particularly frequent in subgroups of affected people.

The generalisability of results from RCTs on the efficacy and safety profile of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies across diverse health systems, particularly in relation to the requirements for their optimal and safe management, is currently debated [27]. The cost of these novel drugs, their administration, and the implementation of programmes to minimise the risk of adverse events may be challenging in many healthcare settings. The findings of the review could therefore be an important support for decision‐making in clinical practice.

Positions by health decision makers regarding amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies are discordant. Like the Food and Drug Agency and the European Medicines Agency, the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency approved lecanemab for MCI and mild dementia due to AD [36]. However, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence expressed a negative opinion about its reimbursement by the UK NHS (National Health System) since benefits are too small to justify the cost to the NHS [37]. The same opinion was expressed about donanemab [38]. Moreover, the Dutch Health Institute (Zorginstitut Nederland) refused reimbursement of lecanemab for patients with MCI or mild dementia due to AD, based on the absence of net benefit. The Institute concluded that desirable effects on cognition are very small in the face of potentially serious side effects [39]. Recently, the German Institute for Quality and Efficiency in German Healthcare (IQWIG) concluded that lecanemab does not offer benefit to patients with MCI or early dementia due to AD [40].

Numerous systematic reviews have been conducted on amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies for MCI and mild dementia due to AD, although none of them are Cochrane reviews. Their main limitations are being focused on only one agent [41, 42]; focusing only on efficacy [43, 44] or safety [32, 45]; methodological weaknesses [41, 42]; or including medications from drug classes other than amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies in pooled analysis [46]. Some review authors point out that, despite the statistical significance of results, whether the magnitude of their effect is clinically important remains uncertain [47], while others report the absence of a positive effect [43, 44]. A recently published national guideline developed by means of the GRADE methodology strongly recommends against the use of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies [48].

Given the rapidly evolving landscape of AD therapeutics and the significant implications for patients, caregivers, healthcare systems, and society, a comprehensive and up‐to‐date systematic review is particularly relevant now and is needed to appraise and synthesise current evidence on the efficacy and safety of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies in people with MCI or mild dementia due to AD.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To assess the clinical benefits and harms of the amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies aducanumab, bapineuzumab, crenezumab, donanemab, gantenerumab, lecanemab, ponezumab, remternetug, and solanezumab in people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We followed the Methodological expectations of Cochrane intervention reviews (MECIR) when conducting the review [49], and PRISMA 2020 for the reporting [50].

sec.title: Differences between protocol and review
sec.p: We modified our objectives to add remternetug to the list of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies.

We extracted the number of people with "any ARIA E" and "any ARIA H" separately, instead of extracting the number of "any ARIA" as stated in the protocol, since most of the included studies did not report the total number of people with ARIA.

sec.title: Criteria for considering studies for this review
Types of studies
sec.p: In this review, we included individual, parallel‐group, phase‐3 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and cluster‐RCTs of at least 12 months' duration that investigated amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies for the treatment of people with MCI or mild dementia due to AD. Phase‐3 RCTs can provide confirmatory evidence of efficacy and safety of interventions and are the most informative for clinical practice, regulatory decision‐making, and guideline development. Due to the clinical characteristics and the chronic, progressive course of AD, studies with follow‐up shorter than 12 months may not capture the benefits and harms of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies. Therefore, we excluded trials that lasted less than 12 months.
We did not include cross‐over trials since such a design is inappropriate for studies in AD due to the degenerative, progressive nature of the condition.

We included abstracts only if sufficient information was available on study design and characteristics of participants, interventions, and outcomes to allow extraction of data and evaluation of the risk of bias.

sec.title: Types of participants
sec.p: We included adult participants (aged 18 years or older) with a clinical diagnosis of:

list-item.p: mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to probable or possible AD according to the criteria of the National Institute on Aging‐Alzheimer's Association workgroups on diagnostic guidelines for Alzheimer's disease [6, 51, 52] or of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders Fifth Edition [53]; or

mild dementia due to probable or possible AD according to the criteria of the National Institute on Aging‐Alzheimer's Association workgroups on diagnostic guidelines for AD [10] or of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders fourth and fifth Edition [53, 54].

sec.p: We included studies that had only a subset of eligible participants if data for the subgroup of interest were reported or were provided after we contacted the authors of the study. If we received no response from the study's authors within 15 working days of contacting them, we classified the study as 'awaiting classification'.

sec.title: Types of interventions
sec.p: Experimental intervention: treatment with any of the following amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies: aducanumab, bapineuzumab, crenezumab, donanemab, gantenerumab, lecanemab, ponezumab, remternetug, and solanezumab, at any dose and by parenteral route.

We allowed concomitant use of other medications, either as part of the study (e.g. in a factorial design) or as part of usual care, as long as the use in the intervention and control arm was the same.
Comparison intervention: placebo or no intervention

sec.title: Critical outcomes
sec.p: We considered as critical outcomes the potential benefits and harms outlined in Table 1.

td.bold: Table 1. Specific measures for each critical outcome

tr.td: Change on a validated cognitive instrument, including extended screening instruments in any version, such as the Alzheimer's Disease Assessment Scale‐Cognitive subscale (ADAS‐Cog) [55], the Cambridge Cognitive Examination (CamCog) [56]; cognitive screening tools, like the Mini Mental State Examination (MMSE) [57], the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) [58]; individual neuropsychological tests, such as the Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test (RAVLT) [59], or standardised neuropsychological test batteries
Change on the Clinical Dementia Rating Scale Sum of Boxes (CDR‐SB) [60].

Change on a validated instrument for the assessment of activities of daily living (ADL) or instrumental activities of daily living (IADL), appropriate for the disease stage, including the ADL [61] and IADL scales [62], the Disability Assessment for Dementia (DAD) scale [63], the Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study – Activities of Daily Living scale (ADCS‐ADL) and its adaptation for people with MCI (ADCS‐ADL Mild Cognitive Impairment scale (ADCS‐ADL‐MCI)) [64, 65]

Number of participants presenting any ARIA E or any ARIA H, defined as a spectrum of MRI findings related to amyloid‐lowering treatments encompassing vasogenic oedema or effusions (ARIA E), and haemorrhagic changes (ARIA H)
Number of participants presenting with symptomatic ARIA E or ARIA H
Number of participants with symptomatic brain haemorrhage
Number of participants with any (one or more) SAEs, defined according to the authors of the study
Number of deaths by any cause

sec.title: Important outcomes
sec.p: We considered as important outcomes the potential benefits and harms as outlined in Table 2.

td.bold: Table 2. Specific measures for each important outcome

tr.td: Change on a validated instrument, including the Mild Behavioural Impairment Checklist (MBI‐C) [66], the Neuropsychiatric Inventory Questionnaire (NPI‐Q) [67], and the Neurobehavioral Rating Scale [68]
Number of participants with local or systemic reactions to the infusion of the intervention or comparator
Treatment discontinuation at the end of the study

sec.p: We included outcomes assessed at 12 months, from above 12 months to 18 months, from above 18 months to 24 months, and above 24 months, as reported in the included studies.
Outcomes were not used as inclusion criteria for the review.

sec.title: Search methods for identification of studies
Electronic searches
sec.p: The Information Specialist identified eligible study references through systematic searches of the following bibliographic databases.

list-item.p: The Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) (from inception to 2025, Issue 8)
MEDLINE (PubMed) (from inception to 7 August 2025)
Embase (Embase.com) (from inception to 7 August 2025)

sec.p: We also searched two clinical trials registries: Clinicaltrials.gov and the WHO International Clinical Trials Registry Platform [69].

We did not apply any language restriction to the search. We used the Cochrane Highly Sensitive Search Strategy for identifying randomised trials for each bibliographic database, according to the methods recommended in Chapter 4 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [70]. The most recent search date was 7 August 2025.

sec.title: Searching other resources
sec.p: To identify other relevant studies, we screened reference lists of review articles on the topic, as well as reference lists of included studies.

We examined any relevant retraction statements and errata for the included studies following the rules described in the Technical Supplement to Chapter 4: Searching for and selecting studies of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [70]. We also checked the Retraction Watch Database.
Our preliminary search strategy (on MEDLINE‐PubMed database, see Supplementary material 1) was adapted for use in the other electronic bibliographic databases.

sec.title: Selection of studies
sec.p: Three pairs of review authors (from MGL, CB, CDS, LS, EB, LV) independently screened the citations and abstracts identified by the search. We used Covidence software for screening [71]. We retrieved full‐text reports for potentially eligible studies, and the same pairs of review authors independently screened them for eligibility. Two review authors (FN, GF) resolved any disagreements at both screening stages.

If necessary, we contacted study investigators to obtain or confirm relevant information concerning the inclusion and exclusion criteria. When there was no response from study investigators, we sent a reminder letter. When there was no response within 15 working days, we categorised the study as 'awaiting classification'.
We recorded the selection process in sufficient detail to complete a PRISMA flow diagram and a 'Characteristics of excluded studies' table.

sec.title: Data extraction and management
sec.p: We extracted data onto a pre‐designed form on a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet. We piloted the form on four studies.

Two pairs of review authors (from MGL, SM, CDS, LV) independently extracted study information. The review authors were not blinded to authors, institutions, or journals. We resolved any disagreement about data extraction by referring to the study documents and discussing them as a review team. If necessary, we contacted the published trial authors to clarify or provide additional information.
We extracted the following study characteristics.

list-item.p: Methods: setting, number of centres and location, dates, total duration, trial design, characteristics of trial design as outlined in Risk of bias assessment in included studies

Participants: number of participants randomised, mean age, age range, sex/gender, disease duration, severity of the condition, diagnostic criteria, cardiovascular disease as relevant comorbidity, inclusion and exclusion criteria
Interventions: type of drug, doses, and frequencies, duration of treatment; comparison intervention, concomitant medications, and excluded medications
Outcomes: critical and important outcomes, measures, and time points
Notes: funding for trials and notable declarations of interest of trial authors
Information needed to conduct GRADE assessments (e.g. baseline risk in the control group for key outcomes)

sec.p: Two pairs of review authors (from MGL, SM, CDS, LV) independently extracted outcome data from the included studies.

We extracted the number of events and participants per treatment group for dichotomous outcomes, as well as means and standard deviations (SDs), and the number of participants per treatment group for continuous outcomes. We noted in the 'Characteristics of included studies' table if outcome data were not reported in a usable way, as well as when we transformed or estimated data from a graph. We resolved any disagreement about data extraction by referring to the study documents and discussing them as a review team.
We extracted the following outcome data.

list-item.p: If both final and change‐from‐baseline values were reported for the same outcome, we extracted change‐from‐baseline data. If changes from baseline were not reported, we computed them by subtracting endpoint and baseline data, if provided.
If both unadjusted and adjusted values for the same outcome were reported, we extracted the adjusted values.
If available, we extracted data based on an intention‐to‐treat (ITT) sample.

If multiple time points were reported, we extracted data from the latest time point reported in each of four time periods: 12 months, from above 12 months up to and including 18 months, from above 18 months up to and including 24 months, and above 24 months.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment in included studies
sec.p: Two pairs of review authors (from MGL, SM, GF, EB) independently assessed the risk of bias of RCTs using the Cochrane risk of bias tool RoB 2 [72], as described in Chapter 8 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [73]. We resolved any disagreements by discussion.
This review focused on the effect of assignment to the intervention (i.e. the ITT effect).
For each included trial and each critical outcome, we assessed the following domains of bias.

list-item.p: Bias arising from the randomisation process
Bias due to deviations from intended interventions
Bias due to missing outcome data
Bias in measurement of the outcome
Bias in selection of the reported result

sec.p: We assessed the risk of bias for the following outcomes of critical importance that were reported in our summary of findings table: cognitive function measured by means of the ADAS‐CoG scale; dementia severity measured by means of the CDR‐SB scale; functional ability measured by means of the ACDS‐ADL, ACDS‐iADL, ACDS‐ADL‐MCI scales; and the following safety outcomes: any ARIA E and ARIA H, any symptomatic ARIA E and ARIA H, symptomatic brain haemorrhage, SAEs, mortality by any cause, at the end of treatment or the last follow‐up measurement time point, which was at 12 months, between above 12 months and 18 months, between above 18 months and 24 months, or above 24 months.

We judged each domain as 'low risk of bias', 'high risk of bias', or 'some concerns'. The judgements were facilitated by an algorithm that maps responses to the signalling questions, with options of 'yes', 'probably yes', 'probably no', 'no', and 'no information'. We presented an overall risk of bias judgement for each result based on our assessments across the five domains, which was no better than the worst risk of bias assessment in any one of the domains. We judged the overall risk of bias as 'low risk of bias', 'high risk of bias', or 'some concerns', according to the criteria in the current Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [74]. We performed and documented the risk of bias assessment using the RoB 2 Excel tool.

We used our overall risk of bias assessment for each result in the review to inform our GRADE assessment (see Certainty of the evidence assessment) and for sensitivity analysis (see Sensitivity analysis).
Had we found cluster‐RCTs, we would have used the RoB 2 tool for cluster‐randomised trials (https://www.riskofbias.info/welcome/rob-2-0-tool/rob-2-for-cluster-randomized-trials).

sec.title: Measures of treatment effect
sec.p: For dichotomous outcomes, we calculated risk ratios (RR) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). For continuous outcomes, we estimated the standardised mean difference (SMD) with 95% CI.

sec.title: Unit of analysis issues
sec.p: When a single trial had multiple arms with different doses, we included only the highest dose in our analysis.

Had we found cluster‐RCTs, we would have analysed them according to methods described in Chapter 23 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [75]. If studies did not control for clustering, we would have imputed the intracluster correlation coefficient (ICC) from similar studies. We would have divided the effective sample size of each arm by the 'design effect' quantity (1 + (M − 1) x ICC), where M is the average cluster size.

sec.title: Dealing with missing data
sec.p: For all outcomes, we carried out analyses on an ITT basis as far as possible. We examined the extent of and reasons for missing data as part of our risk of bias assessment for each included study. We did not contact trial authors to obtain any missing data. In order to assess the effect of missing outcome data, we also analysed dichotomous data according to a likely scenario, that is, we assumed that treated and control group participants who contributed to missing outcome data both had an unfavourable outcome (see Sensitivity analysis).
For dichotomous data, we used the number of participants analysed in the original study as a denominator.

For continuous outcomes, we calculated the SMD based on the number of participants analysed at that time point. If the number of participants analysed was not presented for each time point, we used the number of randomised participants in each group at baseline. Where possible, we calculated missing SDs from other statistics, such as standard errors, CIs, or P values. When none of the above methods allowed calculation of the SDs from the trial report, we borrowed the SD from one or more other studies, according to the methods recommended in Chapter 6 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [76].

sec.title: Reporting bias assessment
sec.p: When 10 or more studies were available for meta‐analysis, we investigated reporting biases (such as publication bias) by means of funnel plots. We made an assessment of funnel plot asymmetry visually.

sec.title: Synthesis methods
sec.p: We performed standard pairwise meta‐analyses for every comparison with at least two trials, using Cochrane's Review Manager (RevMan) software [77]. We used the random‐effects model because we assumed that the included studies were estimating different, yet related, intervention effects [78].

We used the inverse‐variance approach, incorporating a measure of the extent of heterogeneity into the study weights. In particular, we used the restricted maximum likelihood (REML) to estimate the between‐study variance [79].

As advised in Chapter 10 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [80], we calculated the CIs by means of the Wald‐type confidence interval method in the case of one or two studies and the Hartung‐Knapp‐Sidik‐Jonkman method in all other cases.

We conducted the following comparison: any amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibody at the highest assessed dose versus placebo or no intervention at the end of treatment or at the last follow‐up measurement.

We performed class‐level pooling across different amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies. We did this because all of them share a similar mechanism of action by targeting the Aβ, and amyloid‐PET imaging is the measure of target engagement for all of them. In spite of small differences in the epitope of the amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibody that is targeted, the effect of these drugs can therefore be evaluated as one class of drugs.

sec.title: Investigation of heterogeneity and subgroup analysis
sec.p: We pooled data if studies were clinically homogeneous in respect of the study population, interventions, and outcomes. We assessed the presence of clinical heterogeneity within each pairwise comparison by comparing the trial and study population characteristics across all eligible trials. We assessed statistical heterogeneity in each meta‐analysis by visually inspecting the forest plot and using the Tau2, I2, and Chi2 statistics. We did not calculate prediction intervals. We used the I2 statistic to assess statistical heterogeneity, interpreting it as follows.

list-item.p: 0% to 40% heterogeneity might not be important.
30% to 60% may represent moderate heterogeneity.
50% to 90% may represent substantial heterogeneity.
75% to 100% represents considerable heterogeneity.

sec.p: These overlapping intervals reflect that the interpretation of the I2 statistic depends on the size and direction of the treatment effect, and variance of the I2 estimate. We did not pool results with considerable heterogeneity (I2 of 75% or above).
We performed the following subgroup analyses for all critical and important outcomes with sufficient data.

list-item.p: Type of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibody
Level of cognitive function: MCI versus mild dementia (to be in the respective subgroups, studies had to have at least 70% of participants diagnosed at inclusion with MCI or mild dementia.)

sec.p: We had also planned to perform subgroup analysis based on the comparator: placebo versus no treatment, but we did not do this because all studies compared treatment versus placebo.

We used the formal test for subgroup interactions in RevMan [77]. We interpreted subgroup analyses cautiously, as advised in Chapter 10 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [80]. We compared the magnitude of the effects between the subgroups by assessing the overlap of the CIs of the summary estimate, with non‐overlap of the CIs indicating statistical significance.

sec.title: Equity‐related assessment
sec.p: We assessed health equity by means of the PROGRESS framework [92] and following guidance provided by the PRO EDI initiative (PRO EDI initiative) [93, 94]. We identified potentially overlooked populations by extracting recommended data in the participant characteristics table relative to age, sex/gender, race/ethnicity/ancestry, socioeconomic status, level of education, and location (Supplementary material 2).
We interpreted and discussed the availability of health equity data and their distribution across included studies under Equity assessment.

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: In order to assess the robustness of the synthesised results, we conducted the following sensitivity analyses for all critical and important outcomes.

list-item.p: We included only study participants in whom PET or CSF testing for amyloid was abnormal (these may be entire study populations, or subgroups in mixed RCTs).
We excluded studies at an overall high risk of bias.
We analysed dichotomous data according to a likely scenario, that is, we assumed that treated and control group participants who contributed to missing outcome data both had an unfavourable outcome.

sec.title: Certainty of the evidence assessment
sec.p: We followed the guidelines in Chapters 14 and 15 of the Cochrane Handbookfor Systematic Reviews of Interventions for results [81, 82], taking care to distinguish a lack of evidence of effect from a lack of effect.
We created a summary of findings (SoF) table and presented the following critical outcomes measured at the end of treatment.

list-item.p: Cognitive function measured by ADAS‐Cog
Dementia severity measured by the Clinical Dementia Rating Scale (CDR‐SB)

Functional ability measured by the Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study – Activities of Daily Living scale (ADCS‐ADL), the Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study – Activities of Daily Living Mild Cognitive Impairment scale (ADCS‐ADL‐MCI), and the Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study – Instrumental Activities of Daily Living scale (ACSD‐iADL)
Any amyloid‐related imaging abnormality oedema (ARIA E)
Symptomatic ARIA E
Any amyloid‐related imaging abnormality haemorrhage (ARIA H)
Symptomatic ARIA H
Symptomatic brain haemorrhage
Serious adverse events (SAEs)
Any‐cause mortality

sec.p: In SoF tables, we presented the comparison of any amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibody at the highest assessed dose versus placebo, reporting results at between 12 and 18 months, between above 18 months and 24 months, and above 24 months, depending on treatment duration of the studies. We present our main Table 1 in the body of the review, and a second SoF table for time points above 18 months in Supplementary material 11.

We used the five GRADE domains (study limitations or overall risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision, indirectness, and publication bias) to assess the certainty of a body of evidence as it relates to the studies that contributed data to the analyses of the prespecified outcomes; we reported the certainty of evidence as high, moderate, low, or very low [83].

list-item.p: Risk of bias: we considered the study's contribution (i.e. 'weight') to the pooled estimate. If more than 50% of the weight in the overall estimate came from studies at an overall high risk of bias, or if all the studies were judged as at overall unclear risk of bias, we downgraded the certainty of the evidence by one level. If more than 75% of the weight in the overall estimate came from studies at an overall high risk of bias, we downgraded by two levels.

Imprecision: for each critical outcome, we assessed whether the CIs of the pooled estimates of absolute effect crossed predefined thresholds between trivial/no effect, small, moderate, and large effect, according to methods recommended in the Cochrane Handbook and by the GRADE Working Group guidance [81, 84]. Estimates suggesting a large effect with no apparent imprecision were further tested by assessing if the Review Information Size (RIS, also called Optimal Information Size, OIS) was met, and if not, we considered rating down for imprecision [83, 85, 86]. According to this approach, we downgraded the certainty of evidence by one, two, or three levels, depending on the number of thresholds that were crossed by the 95% CI of the absolute estimate of effect [84, 87]. For critical continuous outcomes, we computed the SMD to estimate the effect size, and our judgement about imprecision was based on 'Cohen’s d' thresholds for assessing the magnitude of effect (0.2 as threshold between trivial to small effect, 0.5 between small and moderate effect, and 0.8 between moderate and large effect) [88]. For critical dichotomous outcomes, in order to provide a numerical value of clinically important benefit, we defined thresholds between trivial/no effect, small effect, moderate effect, and large effect. For each outcome, we defined such thresholds by combining generic coefficients and the health state utility values (HSUVs) elicited from four clinicians with experience in dementia management (EB, LS, LV, ER) and three caregivers of people with dementia (see Acknowledgements) [84]. HSUVs are a summary index measure of health‐related quality of life used in economic analysis, usually obtained by means of surveys amongst people affected by a condition, asked to assign a value to health states on a scale on which 1 is equivalent to full health and 0 is considered equivalent to being dead [89]. Given the numerical imbalance between clinical experts and caregivers, we computed a weighted mean of the elicited HSUVs by attributing a weight of 1 to each one of the four clinicians and of 1.3 to each one of the three caregivers. See Supplementary material 10.

Indirectness: we considered downgrading the certainty of the evidence by one level if the results were not fully generalisable to all countries/settings/participants; we did not downgrade by two levels for this domain.

Inconsistency: we considered the statistical test for heterogeneity (I2), overlap of CIs, and similarity of point estimates. We considered downgrading by one level if I2 was substantial (between 50% and 75%) and the point estimates of individual studies were in different directions.

Publication bias: as described in the Reporting bias assessment section, when we included 10 or more studies in a meta‐analysis, we evaluated potential publication bias by means of funnel plots. We assessed funnel plot asymmetry visually. If publication bias was suggested by a visual assessment, we downgraded the evidence certainty by one level [83].

sec.p: The GRADE approach uses the following definitions of the certainty of the evidence.

list-item.p: High: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect.
Moderate: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different.
Low: our confidence in the effect estimate is limited: the true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect.
Very low: we have very little confidence in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect.

sec.p: Two review authors (FN, SM) independently assessed the certainty of the evidence, with any disagreements resolved by discussion or by involving a third review author (GF). We used GRADEpro GDT software to prepare the SoF tables [90]. We reported and justified all decisions to downgrade the certainty of the evidence in footnotes. When pooled analysis was not possible due to high heterogeneity, the assessment of certainty of the evidence across the five GRADE domains (methodological limitations of the studies or risk of bias, indirectness, imprecision, inconsistency, and the likelihood of publication bias) was considered narratively by two review authors (FN, SM) [91].

sec.title: Consumer involvement
sec.p: We involved consumers in this review by means of a closed recruitment [95]. We contacted caregivers of people with MCI or mild dementia by posting a task on the Cochrane citizen science platform Cochrane Engage, asking them to rate utility values for dichotomous outcomes and to provide suggestions and comments during the development of the review's plain language summary. We collected input from three consumers through email (Supplementary material 9).

sec.title: Results of the search
sec.p: Through our bibliographic database searches, we identified 12,146 records after de‐duplication (see Figure 1 for PRISMA flow diagram of the study selection process). We screened all records and retrieved the full‐text papers of 178 potentially relevant articles. After screening and checking the full texts, we included 17 studies that were published in 56 records [96, 97, 98, 99, 100, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107]. We classified six further studies as ongoing and two as 'awaiting classification'.
