Critical:‐Dementia severity‐Cognitive function‐Functional ability‐Amyloid‐related imaging abnormality‐Symptomatic ARIA E or ARIA H‐Serious adverse events‐MortalityImportant:‐Infusion reaction‐Treatment discontinuation

fn.p: *MCI: mild cognitive impairment; MD: mild dementia
§PET: positron emission tomography; CSF: cerebrospinal fluid

sec.title: Study designs and settings
sec.p: All 17 included studies were RCTs with parallel‐group randomisation. We did not find any cluster‐RCTs. The 17 studies included a total of 20,342 participants.

The studies were conducted between 2014 and 2024, with durations varying from 18 months to 27 months. The studies were performed in multicentre settings, and took place in different countries, including Argentina, Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Bulgaria, Canada, Chile, Costa Rica, Croatia, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Hungary, Italy, Japan, Lithuania, Mexico, Netherlands, New Zealand, Poland, Portugal, Republic of Korea, Russia, Serbia, Slovakia, Slovenia, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, Turkey, Ukraine, the UK, and the USA.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: An overview of the characteristics of the included participants is provided in Table 2 and Supplementary material 2.

The mean age of the participants varied from 69.5 years [102] to 73.9 years [104]. The percentage of female participants in the studies ranged from 50% [97] to 64.5% [108]. Gender identity was not reported in any study, although the terms 'sex' and 'gender' were frequently used interchangeably to refer to biological sex.

The distribution of cognitive subtypes varied across studies. Seven studies enroled only participants with mild dementia [98, 100, 103, 104, 107, 108, 109], and one study included only participants with MCI [102]. In the remaining nine studies with a mixed population, the distribution ranged from 15.7% MCI and 84.3% mild dementia [105] to 83% MCI and 17% mild dementia [97]. One study did not report the distribution [99].
The mean duration of cognitive impairment ranged from 16.8 ± 18 months [106] to 51.6 ± 31.2 months [98].

No study reported participants' cardiovascular disease comorbidities. Fifteen studies (88.2%) included participants with concomitant use of donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine, or memantine. One study explicitly excluded participants requiring memantine or acetylcholinesterase inhibitors [102], and one study did not provide information about this [99].

Race, ethnicity, or ancestry were comprehensively reported in 14 studies (82.4%). Three studies reported only the proportion of white participants [100, 103, 109], and one study mentioned race in the methods without reporting any data [102]. Across the studies, the mean percentage of white participants was 83.8%. No study presented a mean percentage of white participants below 75%, and four studies reported more than 90% white participants [98, 103, 105, 109]. Ethnicity categorised as Hispanic/Latino versus non‐Hispanic/Latino was reported in six studies [96, 97, 99, 105, 106, 110, 111], with reported mean values ranging from 2% [111] to 23.7% [110].
No study reported markers of socioeconomic status.
Mean years of participant education ranged from 11.8 ± 3.8 [107] to 14.7 ± 3.7 [111]. Five studies (23.5%) did not provide information on participants’ educational level [99, 100, 103, 106, 109].

sec.title: Interventions and comparisons
sec.p: Three studies assessed aducanumab [97, 99, 111]; four assessed bapineuzumab [103, 107, 108, 109]; two assessed crenezumab [101, 112]; one assessed donanemab [105]; four assessed gantenerumab [96, 100, 102, 110]; one assessed lecanemab [106]; and two assessed solanezumab [98, 104].
All studies compared the active medication with a placebo group.

sec.title: Critical outcomes (desirable)
sec.p: All studies reported an assessment of cognitive function with the Alzheimer's Disease Assessment Scale‐Cognitive subscale (ADAS‐Cog) [55]. Ten studies also used the Mini Mental State Examination (MMSE [57]) [97, 98, 99, 100, 101, 102, 104, 105, 111, 112].
Thirteen studies assessed dementia severity with the Clinical Dementia Rating Scale Sum of Boxes (CDR‐SB [60]) [96, 97, 98, 99, 100, 101, 102, 104, 105, 106, 110, 111, 112].

Eight studies assessed functional ability with the Alzheimer's Disease Cooperative Study – Activities of Daily Living (ADCS‐ADL [64]) [96, 96, 97, 100, 105, 110, 110, 111], and four studies with ADCS‐ADL Mild Cognitive Impairment scale (ADCS‐ADL‐MCI [65]) [97, 99, 106, 111]. Four studies used the Disability Assessment for Dementia scale (DAD [63]) [103, 107, 108, 109].

sec.title: Critical outcomes (undesirable)
sec.p: Two studies reported the number of participants with any amyloid‐related imaging abnormality (ARIA) [105, 106].

All studies reported the number of participants with any ARIA E (symptomatic or asymptomatic), and four studies reported symptomatic ARIA E [96, 105, 106, 110]. Nine studies reported any ARIA H [96, 99, 100, 101, 102, 105, 106, 110, 112], and only one study reported symptomatic ARIA H [106].
Fifteen studies reported the number of participants with SAEs [96, 97, 98, 99, 100, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 109, 110, 111, 112].
Thirteen studies reported on mortality [96, 98, 99, 100, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 109, 110, 112].
The number of participants with symptomatic brain haemorrhages was not reported.

sec.title: Important outcomes
sec.p: Improvement in behavioural symptoms assessed with the Neuropsychiatric Inventory Questionnaire (NPI‐Q [67]) was reported in three studies [99, 101, 112].

Regarding undesirable important outcomes, infusion reaction was reported in four studies [101, 105, 106, 112], while treatment discontinuation at the end of the study was reported in 15 studies [96, 97, 98, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 110, 111, 112].

sec.title: Treatment duration and follow‐up
sec.p: Treatment duration lasted less than 18 months in one study [112], between 18 and 24 months in 14 studies [97, 98, 99, 100, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 111], and more than 24 months in two studies [96, 110].

In terms of our analysis time periods, the longest follow‐up measurement was from 12 to 18 months in 11 studies [97, 98, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 111, 112], from above 18 months to 24 months in four studies [99, 100, 101, 102], and more than 24 months in two studies [96, 110].
Nine studies were terminated: six for futility[97, 100, 101, 102, 111, 112], two for high rates of clinically symptomatic ARIA E [107, 108], and one did not report the reason for early stopping [99].

sec.title: Funding and conflicts of interest
sec.p: All 17 studies were funded by the pharmaceutical industry.

sec.title: Ongoing studies
sec.p: We identified six ongoing randomised, double‐blind trials evaluating amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies against placebo in populations at risk for or with early AD. Cognitive outcomes are being assessed in five of the studies [113, 114, 115, 116, 117], and dementia severity measures are included in four of the studies [113, 114, 115, 118]. ARIA are being specifically assessed in two studies [113, 116], and other safety outcomes assessed in three studies [113, 114, 118].
Donanemab is being assessed by three studies [115, 117, 118], lecanemab [113] and remternetug [116] by one study each, and solanezumab and gantenerumab are being assessed within the same study [114].

Three studies are reported as having completed participant recruitment [114, 116, 118], while one study is currently recruiting [115], and one has not yet begun recruitment [117]. Another study is listed as active, but its recruitment status is unclear, being reported as either ongoing or completed [113].
One study is not funded by the pharmaceutical industry [117], while four are funded by Eli Lilly and Company [114, 115, 116, 118], and one by Eisai [113].

sec.title: Studies awaiting classification
sec.p: Two studies are classified as awaiting classification. One is an ongoing Japanese registry study [119]. This study is evaluating intravenous crenezumab compared with placebo in participants with probable AD dementia and reports serious adverse events as outcomes; classification is pending because no results have been posted to date. The second study is Joseph‐Mathurin 2024 [120] [120], which is reporting data from the DIAN‐TU‐001 randomised controlled trial of gantenerumab and solanezumab, which assessed cognitive outcomes, dementia severity, and ARIA; classification is pending because outcomes were reported only as pooled data of participants with symptomatic early dementia or without cognitive symptoms. We contacted the corresponding author on 24 October 2025, asking for relevant population data, but we have received no reply.
See Supplementary material 4 and Supplementary material 5 for more details of the ongoing and not‐yet‐classified studies.

sec.title: Excluded studies
sec.p: We excluded 114 records. The main reasons for exclusion were as follows: ineligible patient population (19 records), ineligible comparison or intervention (12 records), ineligible outcomes (18 records), short‐term follow‐up (26 records), ineligible publication type (18 records), and ineligible study design (21 records). Some studies had more than one reason for exclusion (Supplementary material 3).

sec.title: Risk of bias in included studies
sec.p: We assessed the risk of bias in the outcomes that we reported in our summary of findings table, using the RoB 2 tool [72]. The results of our assessment can be found in the RoB 2 tables (Supplementary material 6) and on the forest plots of the analyses. Detailed assessments, including our responses to the signalling questions, are available from the corresponding author (FN) on reasonable request. Below, we provide a summary of the assessment.
Overall, our assessment of the risk of bias was very similar across all the included studies.

For four studies [97, 99, 104, 111], we evaluated the overall risk of bias as "some concerns" due to the randomisation process domain, as the methods for random sequence generation or allocation concealment (or both) were not described.

All of the studies were double‐blind and provided an adequate description of the methods used to guarantee blinding of participants, providers, and outcome assessors; however, for the efficacy outcomes, we judged the overall risk of bias as "some concerns" for all studies, due to bias in measurement of the outcomes, as adverse effects (i.e. infusion reaction) were much more frequent in the treated arm, which could lead to functional unblinding. We judged the safety outcomes ARIA E and ARIA H to be at low risk of bias for measurement of the outcomes, as the radiologists who read the MRIs were blind to treatment assignment in all the studies. We also judged the safety outcomes of serious adverse events and overall mortality to be at low risk of bias for measurement of the outcomes, as the outcomes were objective and unlikely to be biased by functional unblinding.
For three studies [99, 107, 108], we evaluated the overall risk of bias as "some concerns" due to selection of the reported results, as they did not provide a statistical analysis plan.

sec.title: Synthesis of results
sec.p: See Table 1 for detailed descriptions of evidence synthesis for key comparisons and outcomes. Additional results can be found in Supplementary material 2, Supplementary material 7, and Supplementary material 8.

The time points of interest are 18 months (which represents measurements taken any time from 12 months and 1 day up to and including 18 months), 24 months (representing 18 months and 1 day up to and including 24 months), and above 24 months.

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little to no difference in cognitive function, as measured by the ADAS‐Cog scale, compared with placebo at 18 months (SMD −0.11, 95% CI −0.16 to −0.06; I² = 10%; 13 studies, 9895 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 1.1; Figure 2), where higher scores indicate greater cognitive impairment.

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on cognitive function at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in slightly less decline in cognitive function, as measured by the ADAS‐Cog scale, compared with control at 24 months, but the evidence is very uncertain (SMD −0.21, 95% CI −0.81 to 0.38; I² = 68%; 2 studies, 212 participants; very low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 1.2), where higher scores indicate greater cognitive impairment (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in little to no difference in cognitive function, as measured by the ADAS‐Cog scale, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (SMD −0.12, 95% CI −0.21 to −0.03; I² = 0%; 2 studies, 1959 participants; low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 1.3), where higher scores indicate greater cognitive impairment (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in little to no difference in dementia severity, as measured by the CDR‐SB scale, compared with placebo at 18 months (SMD −0.12, 95% CI −0.24 to −0.00; I² = 67%; 9 studies, 8053 participants; low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 2.1; Figure 3), where higher scores indicate more severe dementia.

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on dementia severity at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in little to no effect on dementia severity, as measured by the CDR‐SB scale, compared with placebo at 24 months, but the evidence is very uncertain (SMD 0.03, 95% CI −0.96 to 1.02; I² = 0%; 2 studies, 220 participants; very low certainty evidence; Analysis 2.2), where higher scores indicate more severe dementia (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little to no effect on dementia severity, as measured by the CDR‐SB scale, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (SMD −0.07, 95% CI −0.16 to 0.02; I² = 0%; 2 studies, 1959 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 2.3), where higher scores indicate more severe dementia (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little to no difference in functional ability, as measured by the ADCS‐ADL scale, compared with placebo at 18 months (SMD 0.09, 95% CI 0.03 to 0.16; I² = 0%; 3 studies, 3478 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 3.2; Figure 4), where lower scores indicate worse functional ability.

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on functional ability at 18 months

sec.p: The intervention may result in a small improvement in functional ability, as measured by the ADCS‐ADL‐MCI scale, compared with placebo at 18 months (SMD 0.23, 95% CI 0.12 to 0.33; I² = 0%; 4 studies, 2802 participants; low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 3.5; Figure 5), where lower scores indicate worse functional ability.

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on functional ability ‐measured with ADCS‐ADL‐MCI‐ at 18 months

sec.p: The intervention may result in a small improvement in instrumental functional ability, as measured by the ADCS‐iADL scale, compared with placebo at 18 months, (SMD 0.21, 95% CI 0.10 to 0.32; I² = not applicable; one study, 1252 participants; low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 3.6; Figure 6), where lower scores indicate worse functional ability.

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on functional ability ‐ measured with ADCS‐iADL‐ at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in little to no difference in functional ability, as measured by the ADCS‐ADL scale, compared with placebo at 24 months, but the evidence is very uncertain (SMD 0.09, 95% CI −3.83 to 4.00; I² = 68%; 2 studies, 221 participants; very low certainty evidence; Analysis 3.3), where lower scores indicate worse functional ability (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little to no difference in functional ability, as measured by the ADCS‐ADL scale, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (SMD 0.07, 95% CI −0.03 to 0.16; I² = 0%; 2 studies, 1972 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 3.4), where lower scores indicate worse functional ability (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in a small increase in the absolute risk of ARIA E, compared with placebo at 18 months (RR 10.02, 95% CI 7.49 to 13.41; I² = 25%; ARD 107 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 77 more to 148 more; 11 studies, 13595 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.2; Figure 7).

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on any Amyloid‐Related Imaging Abnormality edema (ARIA E) at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little to no difference in the absolute risk of ARIA E, compared with placebo at 24 months (RR 7.49, 95% CI 2.98 to 18.84; I² = 12%; ARD 32 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 10 more to 89 more; 4 studies, 2733 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.3; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in a large increase in the absolute risk of ARIA E, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (RR 9.35, 95% CI 4.52 to 19.36; I² = 66%; ARD 229 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 97 more to 505 more; 2 studies, 1939 participants; low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.4; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little to no difference in the absolute risk of symptomatic ARIA E, compared with placebo at 18 months (RR 52.49, 95% CI 40.13 to 68.66; I² = 0%; ARD 29 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 22 more to 38 more; 2 studies, 3522 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.5; Figure 8).

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on symptomatic Amyloid‐Related Imaging Abnormality (ARIA E) at 18 months

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in little to no difference in the absolute risk of symptomatic ARIA E, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (RR 15.55, 95% CI 4.33 to 55.80; I² = 0%; ARD 31 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 7 more to 116 more; 2 studies, 1939 participants; low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.6; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The three studies reporting this outcome showed very heterogeneous results (I2 = 81% in the pooled analysis) [105, 106, 112]; therefore, we reported the results of each individual study: RR 2.31, 95% CI 1.90 to 2.80, 1727 participants [105]; RR 1.91, 95% CI 1.49 to 2.46, 1795 participants [106]; RR 0.85, 95% CI 0.47 to 1.52, 786 participants [112] (Analysis 4.7; Figure 9). We judged the overall certainty of the evidence as moderate.

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on any Amyloid‐Related Imaging Abnormality haemorrhage (ARIA H) at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of ARIA‐H, compared with placebo at 24 months (RR 1.27, 95% CI 0.96 to 1.70; I² = 0%; ARD 17 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 2 fewer to 43 more; 2 studies, 913 participants; high‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.8) (Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in a small increase in the absolute risk of ARIA H, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (RR 1.86, 95% CI 1.52 to 2.29; I² = 0%; ARD 105 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 64 more to 158 more; 2 studies, 1939 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.9; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of symptomatic ARIA H, compared with placebo at 18 months (RR 3.00, 95% CI 0.61 to 14.81; I² = not applicable; ARD 4 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 1 fewer to 31 more; 1 study, 1795 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 4.10; Figure 10).

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on symptomatic Amyloid‐Related Imaging Abnormality haemorrhage (ARIA H) at 18 months

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of serious adverse events, compared with placebo at 18 months (RR 1.04, 95% CI 0.94 to 1.16; I² = 39%; ARD 6 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 10 fewer to 26 more; 9 studies, 11904 participants; high‐certainty evidence; Analysis 5.1; Figure 11).

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on serious adverse events (SAEs) at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of serious adverse events, compared with placebo at 24 months (RR 1.03, 95% CI 0.85 to 1.25; I² = 0%; ARD 4 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 20 fewer to 34 more; 4 studies, 2746 participants; high‐certainty evidence; Analysis 5.2; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of serious adverse events, compared with placebo beyond 24 months (RR 0.82, 95% CI 0.67 to 1.02; I² = 0%; ARD 30 fever events/1000 participants, 95% CI 55 fewer to 3 more; 2 studies, 1959 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 5.3; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: 18 months
sec.p: The intervention results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of death, compared with placebo at 18 months (RR 1.17, 95% CI 0.74 to 1.86; I² = 32%; ARD 2 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 3 fewer to 11 more; 7 studies, 9733 participants; high‐certainty evidence; Analysis 6.1; Figure 12).

p.bold: Forest plot showing the effect of Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo on overall mortality at 18 months

sec.title: 24 months
sec.p: The intervention probably results in little or no difference in the absolute risk of death, compared with placebo at 24 months (RR 1.05, 95% CI 0.47 to 2.37; I² = 8%; ARD 1 more events/1000 participants, 95% CI 6 fever to 16 more; 4 studies, 2746 participants; moderate‐certainty evidence; Analysis 6.2; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Above 24 months
sec.p: The intervention may result in little or no difference in mortality, compared with placebo beyond 24 months, but the evidence is very uncertain (RR 0.70, 95% CI 0.13 to 3.88; I² = 73%; ARD 4 fever events/1000 participants, 95% CI 13 fewer to 42 more; 2 studies, 1959 participants; very low‐certainty evidence; Analysis 6.3; Supplementary material 11).

sec.title: Important outcomes
sec.p: Findings for behavioural symptom improvement (three studies [99, 101, 112]), infusion reactions (four studies [101, 105, 106, 112]), and treatment discontinuation at the end of the study (15 studies [96, 97, 98, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 110, 111, 112]) are provided in Supplementary material 7 (Analysis 7.1; Analysis 7.2; Analysis 8.1; Analysis 8.2; Analysis 9.1; Analysis 9.2; Analysis 9.3).

sec.title: People with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia
sec.p: We conducted separate analyses on the two main subgroups of our population of interest: people with a clinical diagnosis of mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to probable or possible AD. Subgroup analysis did not show significant differences between groups in any of the available efficacy outcomes. See Supplementary material 7 (Analysis 10.1; Analysis 10.1; Analysis 10.1; Analysis 10.2; Analysis 10.2; Analysis 10.2; Analysis 10.3; Analysis 10.3; Analysis 10.3; Analysis 10.4; Analysis 10.4; Analysis 10.4).

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: In order to test the robustness of our results, we performed the following sensitivity analyses.

list-item.p: Including only participants in whom PET or CSF testing for amyloid was abnormal: sensitivity analysis of seven studies where PET or CSF testing revealed abnormal amyloid values showed estimates consistent with those in the primary analysis for all available outcomes [97, 98, 99, 102, 105, 106, 111, 112]. See Supplementary material 7 (Analysis 11.1; Analysis 11.2; Analysis 11.3; Analysis 11.4; Analysis 11.5; Analysis 11.6; Analysis 11.7; Analysis 11.8).

Excluding studies at an overall high risk of bias: only one study had an overall high risk of bias [99]. Sensitivity analysis after excluding this study showed pooled estimates that were consistent with the primary analysis for all outcomes (Analysis 13.1; Analysis 13.3; Analysis 13.4; Analysis 13.5; Analysis 13.6). In both primary and sensitivity analysis, the RR point estimates were very similar and 95% CIs were mostly overlapping (Analysis 13.2). See Supplementary material 7.

Likely scenario: we analysed dichotomous data according to a likely scenario, assuming that participants contributing to missing outcome data in both the intervention and control group had an unfavourable outcome (Analysis 12.1; Analysis 12.10; Analysis 12.11; Analysis 12.12; Analysis 12.13; Analysis 12.14; Analysis 12.15; Analysis 12.16; Analysis 12.17; Analysis 12.18; Analysis 12.2; Analysis 12.3; Analysis 12.4; Analysis 12.5; Analysis 12.6; Analysis 12.7; Analysis 12.8; Analysis 12.9). Results were consistent with the primary analysis for all outcomes but three: "any ARIA E at 24 months" (Analysis 12.3), which showed a higher RR, and "symptomatic ARIA E at 18 months" (Analysis 12.5) and "symptomatic ARIA E above 24 months" (Analysis 12.6), which showed lower RRs. See Supplementary material 7.

sec.title: Equity assessment
sec.p: We assessed equity using the PROGRESS Plus framework. Reporting of equity‐relevant characteristics in the studies was inconsistent, with notable gaps in the description of trial populations. Age, location, and education were generally reported, but no study reported markers of socioeconomic status. The sex of participants was reported in all studies. However, the terms "sex" and "gender" were often used interchangeably, referring to biological sex only. Although most studies reported information on race, white participants consistently represented more than 75% of the studies' populations, while black participants and Hispanic/Latino ethnic groups were markedly under‐represented.

Furthermore, assessment of eligibility for treatment with amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies requires genetic testing and determination of the amyloid load in the brain by investigation of cerebrospinal fluid or amyloid PET scan. Once the treatment is started, monitoring with repeated MRI scans of the brain is required to detect the occurrence of ARIA. Such investigations, particularly in settings with limited resources, may be challenging for health systems and create inequities by limiting access to treatment.

Overall, the available data offer only partial insight into non‐white populations and into patterns of equitable access to treatment, highlighting areas where future research and reporting may be strengthened.

sec.title: Reporting biases
sec.p: We produced funnel plots for the two outcomes for which at least 10 studies were available: cognitive function at 18 months measured by means of the ADAS‐Cog scale and occurrence of any ARIA E at 18 months (Figure 13; Figure 14). From our visual inspection of the plots, we did not consider them to suggest a significant risk of publication bias.

p.bold: Funnel plot of comparison: Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo, outcome: 1.1. Cognitive function at 18 months to check for publication bias
Funnel plot of comparison: Amyloidβ‐targeting monoclonal antibodies versus placebo, outcome: 5.2. Any ARIA E at 18 months to check for publication bias

sec.title: Summary of main results
sec.p: We found 17 eligible placebo‐controlled phase‐3 RCTs on therapy with amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies in MCI or mild dementia due to AD. Most studies investigated the efficacy of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies on cognitive function, severity of dementia, and functional ability (critical outcomes of our review) by means of continuous measures (differences in the scale scores between intervention and control groups). Dementia severity and functional ability were not assessed in all studies. To allow comparison between different continuous outcomes and the contextualisation of their pooled estimates in terms of magnitude of the effect, we expressed all pooled estimates as SMDs. Undesirable events were reported as dichotomous outcomes (number of people presenting the event), and we assessed differences between intervention and control groups as relative risks. Here, we mainly discuss the results at 18 months because treatment lasted 18 months in most studies, so using this time point allowed us to provide more representative and precise estimates.

sec.title: Cognitive function
sec.p: The most widely used tool to measure cognitive function was the ADAS‐Cog scale (range 0 to 70 points). Across 13 studies with a duration of 18 months, the pooled SMD between the intervention and the placebo arms was probably trivial, being below the Cohen's d value of 0.2. In absolute terms, the pooled observed difference in favour of the treatment on the ADAS‐COg scale at 18 months was 0.85 points (moderate certainty). The minimal clinically important difference (MCID) for ADAS‐Cog, according to anchor‐based approaches, is generally considered to be 2 to 3 points in the MCI stage and 4 points in the dementia stage over a 6‐ to 12‐month period [121, 122, 123]. The ADAS‐Cog difference found in the RCTs included in this review is therefore considerably lower over a longer period of time. At longer time points, estimates may be similar, with absolute differences of 1.41 points at 24 months, but the evidence is very uncertain (very low certainty), and of 1.27 points at more than 24 months (low certainty).

sec.title: Dementia severity
sec.p: Dementia severity was assessed at 18, 24, and above 24 months by means of the CDR‐SB scale (range 0 to 18 points). The pooled SMD at 18 months across nine studies may be trivial. In absolute terms, the observed estimate translates into a reduction of 0.29 points (low certainty). The MCID for the CDR‐SB scale, according to anchor‐based approaches, is generally considered to be 1 point in the MCI stage and 2 points in the dementia stage [121]; therefore, the observed difference does not seem to be clinically meaningful. Similar results were observed at 24 months and above 24 months.

sec.title: Functional ability
sec.p: Functional ability was assessed by means of various scales, with most studies using the ADCS‐ADL (range 0 to 78 points) and its variants, ADCS‐iADL (range 0 to 56 points) and ADCS‐MCI (range 0 to 53 points).

At 18 months, a pooled SMD probably in favour of treatment but of trivial magnitude was observed (moderate certainty). Small SMD values were observed in studies where functional ability was assessed at 18 months by means of the ADCS‐ADL‐MCI scale (4 studies) and ADCS‐iADL scale (1 study) (low certainty for both findings). Although no established MCID exists for such scales, absolute differences like those observed, ranging from 0.07 to 1.90 points, do not suggest meaningful clinical improvements on scales with such score ranges.

sec.title: Other outcomes
sec.p: Behavioural symptoms were assessed by means of the NPI‐Q scale in three studies, and no differences between treatment and control groups were found at 18 months. No clear conclusions can therefore be drawn on the effect of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies on behavioural symptoms.

sec.title: ARIA
sec.p: The most common undesirable events associated with the use of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies are ARIA, which at onset can be either symptomatic or, more commonly, asymptomatic. Symptomatic ARIA are particularly threatening, given the potentially severe neurological consequences and the need for immediate assessment and management. Nevertheless, ARIA with asymptomatic onset may also have long‐term undesirable consequences, which are currently uncertain. The reporting of ARIA was inconsistent across studies. Only four studies specifically reported symptomatic ARIA E, and only one study reported symptomatic ARIA H. At 18 months, ARIA (particularly ARIA E) were more common in the treatment groups than in the control groups. Specifically at 18 months, amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies probably resulted in a small absolute increase of any ARIA E and in a trivial increase in the risk of symptomatic ARIA E (moderate certainty for both findings). Overall, at 18 months, ARIA H were probably no more common amongst people treated with amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies than in people receiving a placebo, based on the three studies that reported such data (moderate certainty). However, these studies showed high heterogeneity, and it was not possible to pool their results. Amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies probably resulted in a trivial increase of symptomatic ARIA H (moderate certainty).

Estimates at the longest available follow‐up could be more informative about long‐term safety in a chronic condition like AD. Above 24 months, the pooled estimate of any ARIA E may have suggested a large difference in absolute terms (low certainty), while the estimate of ARIA H probably showed a small increase (moderate certainty). Symptomatic ARIA E may have shown a trivial absolute difference compared to placebo (low certainty), and no data were available on symptomatic ARIA H above 24 months.

APOE ε4 allele homozygosis is associated with a higher risk of developing ARIA. Therefore, we considered a subgroup analysis on APOE ε4 homozygotes versus non homozygotes or non carriers. However, we did not perform it because genetic testing for APOE ε4 status is not part of usual clinical practice, given the substantial impact of test results on patients' offspring, and most of the included studies did not report APOE ε4 status.

sec.title: SAE and mortality
sec.p: SAEs and mortality showed trivial differences between the groups at 18 months (high certainty), as well as at the other time points.

sec.title: Limitations of the evidence included in the review
sec.p: Although the populations investigated in the included studies match the target population of our review (adults with MCI or mild dementia due to AD), the external validity of the study results for the wider AD population is limited. The mean age of trial participants varied between 69.5 and 73.9 years, whereas estimates outside the experimental setting report the mean age of onset to be over 80 years [124]. Due to the strict inclusion criteria, estimates of the AD population potentially eligible for this treatment range from 5% to 15% [125, 126].

We found notable heterogeneity amongst studies in the outcomes of efficacy and in the tools that the study authors used to assess them. Moreover, the timing of the outcome assessment varied, with only six of the 17 studies considering follow‐up duration longer than 18 months [96, 99, 100, 101, 102, 110], which would be desirable in a chronic condition like AD. Such limitations make it challenging to make comparisons between different amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies and to calculate precise pooled estimates, and they hamper the generalisability of the results.

Safety is an important issue when using amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies in clinical practice. An important limitation of the available published literature is the lack of data on symptomatic ARIA, information that is particularly relevant for patients and doctors. Surprisingly, only four studies reported the number of people with symptomatic ARIA E [96, 105, 106, 110], and only one study reported the number of people with symptomatic ARIA H [106].

Including observational cohorts or phase‐IV studies in future reviews may provide further information about the sustained use of the medication and about the risk of adverse events, including ARIA E and ARIA H. Given the relatively short follow‐up in available studies and considering the long disease course of AD, more information on the long‐term safety profile of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies is needed.

Although our assessment of risk of bias across domains found no limitations in the randomisation procedures, we had some concerns with respect to efficacy outcome measures, the magnitude of which may have been substantially impacted by functional unblinding. In some studies (Analysis 8.1; Analysis 8.2), participants in the amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies groups experienced side effects considerably more often than in the placebo groups, causing symptoms or prompting changes in the treatment regimen that could lead to unblinding of study participants and their caregivers to their allocated treatment. Such functional unblinding may have influenced outcome measures of cognition, dementia severity, and functional ability, hampering the validity of such estimates. In particular, for outcomes that are partly informant‐based, such as the CDR‐SB, functional unblinding could have biased the result towards a more positive rating. An attempt to quantify this bias was made by Wolters and colleagues [34], but the analyses were based on three studies [97, 105, 106] and cannot be directly extrapolated to all 17 studies included in our review. Because of the trivial differences in efficacy outcomes between treatment and placebo arms observed across trials, any impact of functional unblinding could further attenuate this difference towards the null. For this reason, while implementing the GRADE methodology, we downgraded our assessment of the certainty of efficacy outcomes by one level for the risk‐of‐bias domain.

All included studies were funded by the pharmaceutical industry, which, in most cases, had control over crucial stages, such as the analysis and interpretation of data, and writing of the final manuscript. In some studies, all co‐authors were employees of the company manufacturing the drug being tested (see Supplementary material 2 for more details). Sponsor‐related influence on the reported results, particularly in relation to the lack of data on ARIA, cannot be excluded and deserves further investigation in future reviews by means of specific tools.

AD is a very common disorder. In several of the trials, the total number of participants included per participating centre was low, suggesting possible selection bias. Most studies included predominantly white populations in Europe and North America, limiting the external validity for people of other ethnicities.

Another limitation of the evidence on amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies is the lack of extractable data on the education level of participants, which would be relevant information, particularly regarding the outcome of cognitive function.

For most of the undesirable outcomes, we downgraded the certainty of the evidence by one or two levels for imprecision because the 95% CIs of the point estimates crossed one or more thresholds between different magnitudes of effect.

sec.title: Limitations of the review processes
sec.p: We used the methods recommended by the Cochrane Handbook and did not identify any major limitation in the review process.

sec.title: Agreements and disagreements with other studies or reviews
sec.p: The small‐to‐absent effects of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies against placebo urge us to reconsider the amyloid cascade hypothesis as the main underlying mechanism of AD. According to this hypothesis, amyloid deposition is a relatively early step in a cascade towards eventual neurodegeneration and the occurrence of symptoms. This would imply that if the therapy was not effective, the pathology may have been too advanced, and interventions should take place even before clinical symptoms occur, which, in turn, would suggest preventive treatment of amyloid‐positive persons without symptoms. Major ethical and societal concerns would arise, and the potential number of eligible individuals would be enormous.

Such a phase‐3 RCT in asymptomatic amyloid‐PET‐positive people has been done with solanezumab, with an intervention and follow‐up period of 240 weeks [127]. No difference was observed between the solanezumab arm and the placebo arm on scales for cognition, activities of daily living, and severity, despite there being less amyloid build‐up in the solanezumab arm. This is in line with a previous meta‐analysis of RCTs on amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies for AD that showed no association between successful amyloid removal and cognitive function [26].

Moreover, a phase‐3 RCT in autosomal dominantly inherited AD comparing gantenerumab or solanezumab to placebo during 4.7 years of treatment did not show benefit for treated patients, in spite of effective amyloid removal from the brain [128].

Several systematic reviews have been developed on the efficacy and safety of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies for AD. Albeit with some inconsistency in methods (inclusion/exclusion criteria, type of analysis, and reporting), the results of recently published reviews with objectives that are similar to those of our review also show relatively small efficacy estimates for cognitive and functional outcomes, together with a higher number of people with ARIA, particularly ARIA E, mostly asymptomatic [47, 129].

sec.title: Implications for practice
sec.p: The effect of amyloid‐beta‐targeting monoclonal antibodies (Aβ‐mAbs) in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) or early dementia due to Alzheimer's disease (AD) on cognitive decline, dementia severity, and functional outcomes over an 18‐month period, is either absent or consistently small. The differences in the main clinical outcome measures, including ADAS‐Cog (Alzheimer's Disease Assessment Scale‐Cognitive) and CDR‐SB (Clinical Dementia Rating Scale Sum of Boxes) scores, are well below the minimal clinically important difference (MCID) on these scales. There are some concerns that the true effect size may be even smaller because of functional unblinding due to common apparent adverse reactions. The risk of adverse events, most notably amyloid‐related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), is high. Although symptomatic ARIA is uncommon, the long‐term consequences of cerebral oedema and recurrent (micro) bleedings are uncertain. The absence of clear reporting of symptomatic ARIA in most studies is a main limitation that precludes a thorough analysis of the true impact of ARIA. The burden on healthcare systems for these treatments, with subsequent inequities in access to care, may be considerable due to the additional resources required for intravenous (IV) administration of the drugs, PET (positron emission tomography) scans, and repeated MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scans to monitor for ARIA, in addition to the cost of the drug.

Given the small effect sizes, with absolute differences well below the MCID, the high risk of ARIA, and the substantial resource burden required for their administration and monitoring, monoclonal antibodies against amyloid‐beta do not offer a favourable benefit to risk balance for patients with MCI or mild dementia due to AD.

sec.title: Equity‐related implications for practice
sec.p: As described above, assessment of eligibility for treatment with monoclonal antibodies against amyloid‐beta requires determination of the amyloid load in the brain by investigation of cerebrospinal fluid or amyloid PET scan. Once the treatment is started, monitoring with repeated MRI scans of the brain is required to detect the occurrence of ARIA. Such investigations, particularly in settings with limited resources, may be burdensome for health systems and create inequities by limiting access to treatment. These challenges may not allow replication of the estimates of efficacy and safety of this class of drugs outside the experimental setting. The use of these drugs seems to be a realistic option only in high‐resource health systems, and even in these settings, the required resources (PET‐scanning, repeated MRI scanning, nursing staff for frequent IV treatment) may not be available to an extent that can ensure equitable access to treatment.

sec.title: Implications for research
sec.p: Future studies should report more transparently on the impact of ARIA. From the current published results, including supplementary materials, in most studies it is not possible to truly know the clinical impact of ARIA. Cerebral oedema and cerebral (micro‐)bleeds are serious conditions and a clear interpretation of the impact on people with AD should be reported in all trials.

The current results make clear that successfully removing amyloid from the brain probably does not result in a clinically meaningful effect on cognitive decline in patients with MCI or early dementia due to AD. New mechanisms of action for treatment of AD, other than aiming for amyloid removal, should be explored. This may include many different approaches, for example, reducing inflammation or investigating the significance of the gut‐brain axis [21].

Given the convincing evidence of the absence of a clinically meaningful effect, in combination with a considerable risk of cerebral oedema and (micro)bleeds in the brain, from the 17 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) included in this review, it is unlikely that future RCTs targeting amyloid‐beta removal will provide clear benefit for people with AD.

In order to ensure generalisability of results, future research on drug treatments for AD should collect data on determinants impacting baseline risk of populations included in the trials, particularly modifiable ones such as socioeconomic status [12].

Future studies should prioritise clinically meaningful outcomes. Sample size calculations should be based on MCIDs, wherever available. Intervention and follow‐up periods should be longer, given the slow rate of decline in the MCI and early dementia stage of AD. More transparent reporting on the occurrence of ARIA and their clinical manifestations (at a minimum, whether or not they are symptomatic) is warranted. While an open‐label extension of an RCT is not suitable to estimate clinical efficacy, it can be useful to determine the long‐term risk of adverse events and to detect rare but severe adverse events that may go unnoticed in relatively short RCTs.

sec.title: Equity‐related implications for research
sec.p: The limited and inconsistent reporting of equity‐related characteristics and the under‐representation of non‐white populations may restrict the assessment of differential treatment effects across groups at highest risk of AD [7, 130]. The inconsistent reporting of educational attainment and the lack of socioeconomic status indicators highlights the need for improved equity‐informed trial design and reporting, in order to consider social determinants in future research on disease‐modifying treatments for AD.

sec.title: Data, code and other materials
sec.p: Data supporting the results of the systematic review, template data extraction forms from Covidence and detailed risk of bias assessment data with consensus responses to the signalling questions in the RoB 2 Excel tool will be made available for users upon request to the review authors.

As part of the published Cochrane review, the following are made available for download for users of the Cochrane Library (see Supplementary material 1: full search strategies for each database; Supplementary material 2: full citation of each unique report for all studies included in the final review; Supplementary material 3: full citation of each unique report for all excluded studies at the full text screen in the final review; Supplementary material 4: full citation of each unique report for all studies awaiting classification in the final review; Supplementary material 5: full citation of each unique report for all ongoing studies in the final review; Supplementary material 6: risk of bias assessments; Supplementary material 7: analysis data, including overall estimates and settings, subgroup estimates, and individual data rows; and Supplementary material 8: data package). Appropriate permissions have been obtained for such use. Analyses and data management were conducted within Cochrane’s authoring tool, RevMan, using the inbuilt computation methods.
